roles: organizer of the early Hasidic movement (in the movement's account)
summary: Dov Ber of Mezeritch (c. 1704-1772), known as the Maggid ('the preacher') of Mezeritch, was the principal disciple and, in the movement's account, the successor of the Baal Shem Tov, whose court trained a cadre of disciples (among them Shneur Zalman of Liady, Levi Yitzchak of Berdichev, and Elimelech of Lizhensk) who carried Hasidism across eastern Europe. He committed almost nothing to writing; his teaching, contemplative and marked by the theme of the annihilation of the self (bittul) before the divine, was gathered by his disciples in the posthumous collection Maggid Devarav le-Yaakov (Korets, 1781).
description:
Dov Ber of Mezeritch, called the Maggid ('the preacher') of Mezeritch after the Volhynian town (Mezhirichi, now in Ukraine) where he held his court, lived c. 1704-1772. In the account of the Hasidic movement he was the leading disciple of Israel ben Eliezer, the Baal Shem Tov, and, after the Besht's death in 1760, his successor at the head of the nascent movement. His birth year is uncertain and is given with c.; before joining the Besht's circle he was, by the tradition's account, a preacher and a scholar versed in the Talmudic and kabbalistic learning of his day.

The historical significance most consistently ascribed to the Maggid in the scholarship is organizational as much as doctrinal. Where the Besht had gathered an intimate circle, the Maggid's court at Mezeritch became a training ground: from it went out a generation of disciples who established Hasidic courts of their own across Volhynia, Podolia, Galicia, Lithuania, and central Poland, among them Shneur Zalman of Liady (founder of the Chabad/Lubavitch school), Levi Yitzchak of Berdichev, Elimelech of Lizhensk, Aaron of Karlin, and Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk. Ada Rapoport-Albert has argued, against the older picture of a single line of succession, that Hasidism after 1772 was carried not by one heir but by this dispersal of disciples into multiple centres, so that the movement's expansion is better understood as structural diffusion than as dynastic inheritance; this reconstruction is recorded here as scholarship. It was in these decades, and partly in reaction to the movement's spread, that the opposition of the mitnagdim (the 'opponents', led from Vilna) crystallized.

The teaching associated with the Maggid, transmitted through his disciples and given here as his and the movement's attributed teaching rather than as claims the editor affirms, is more theoretical and contemplative than the Besht's and is often characterized by scholars as mystical and quietistic in emphasis. Rivka Schatz-Uffenheimer, in the standard study, reads the Maggid's circle as developing a contemplative mysticism turning on the annihilation of the self, the bittul ha-yesh, the nullification of one's own separate 'somethingness' before the divine, and on the recovery of the ayin, the 'nothing' that is the divine ground out of which being is continually contracted. In the disciples' collection the created self is figured as 'dust' that 'can do nothing' of itself, so that its actions are ascribed wholly to God, an acosmic emphasis in which the reality of the separate self and world recedes before the divine All. Cleaving to God (devekut), the contraction (tzimtzum) by which the infinite makes room for the finite, and the ascent of thought to its root are recurrent themes of the homilies gathered under his name.

As with the Besht, a source-critical difficulty attends any account of the Maggid: he wrote no book. His teaching survives in collections compiled by disciples from notes of his oral homilies, chief among them Maggid Devarav le-Yaakov ('The Maggid's Words to Jacob', also known as Likkutei Amarim), assembled by his disciple Shlomo of Lutsk and first printed at Korets in 1781, and the related Or Torah; the Hebrew text of Maggid Devarav le-Yaakov was later given a critical edition by Schatz-Uffenheimer (Jerusalem, 1976). The teaching is therefore read here through the disciples' redaction, attributed to the Maggid as transmitted rather than treated as his own autograph.
editorial_notes: This entry presents the Maggid of Mezeritch on Judaism's own terms as the Baal Shem Tov's principal disciple and, in the movement's account, his successor, and it is HONEST about the same source-critical problem that attends the Besht: the Maggid wrote no book, so the load-bearing source for his teaching is the disciples' posthumous collection Maggid Devarav le-Yaakov (Korets, 1781), read here on open Sefaria. His contemplative and acosmic doctrine (bittul, the annihilation of the separate self; ayin, the divine 'nothing'; tzimtzum; devekut) is ATTRIBUTED to him as transmitted and DESCRIBED, not adjudicated (neutrality #1, #4); the very claim that he 'succeeded' the Besht is given as the movement's account alongside the scholarship, and the picture of a single succession is explicitly qualified by Rapoport-Albert's argument for structural diffusion after 1772. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality #8-#9: (a) the academic historian's standard study of the Maggid's circle, Schatz-Uffenheimer, Hasidism as Mysticism (Princeton/Magnes, 1993), a student of Scholem writing on the Maggid specifically; (b) the tradition's own primary text, open and auditable on Sefaria, Maggid Devarav le-Yaakov, whose section 1 was read line-by-line; (c) a contemporary Jewish scholar and leading historian of the movement, Ada Rapoport-Albert (Hasidism Reappraised; 'Hasidism after 1772'), on the movement's institutionalization and dispersal. No encyclopedias (no Encyclopaedia Judaica, no YIVO Encyclopedia, no Chabad.org, no My Jewish Learning), no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources. CONFIDENCE / HONESTY: Maggid Devarav le-Yaakov on Sefaria is rated HIGH because section 1 was read line-by-line and the load-bearing lines were confirmed verbatim in the open Sefaria Community Translation (CC0), including that God 'contracts Himself' in wisdom which 'is from nothing' (ayin/tzimtzum), that the righteous 'are dust and cannot perform any action without the strength of the Holy One, blessed be He... He alone can do nothing' (the annihilation of the self, bittul, before the divine All), and that Israel 'sustains their Father in Heaven'; the Hebrew is the public-domain Korets 1781 edition. The secondary works (Schatz-Uffenheimer, Rapoport-Albert) are medium, verified to exist and be on-topic via publisher, JSTOR, and Littman/Liverpool University Press records, not read line-by-line. Relation targets: baal-shem-tov and hasidism and kabbalah exist; devekut and shneur-zalman are dangling forward references (flagged in the same batch as dangling); levi-yitzchak-of-berdichev and elimelech-of-lizhensk are dangling forward references to not-yet-created figures.

lifespan.note: c. 1704-1772 CE. The birth year is uncertain and approximate (commonly given as c. 1704-1710) and is entered as 1704; he died in 1772 at Anipoli (Hanipol). He held his court at Mezeritch (Mezhirichi) in Volhynia. Dates are approximate; the reconstruction of the early movement from documentary rather than hagiographic sources is the subject of modern scholarship (Schatz-Uffenheimer, Rapoport-Albert).

relations.note: Dov Ber was the leading disciple of Israel ben Eliezer, the Baal Shem Tov, and in the movement's account his successor; the baal-shem-tov entry exists and records the reciprocal teacher_of edge to maggid-of-mezeritch. How far the Besht designated a successor is treated in the scholarship as the movement's account rather than settled fact.

The Maggid's court at Mezeritch trained the cadre of disciples who spread Hasidism across eastern Europe, and in the standard scholarship the movement's transformation from the Besht's circle into an organized movement is credited to him and to the dispersal of his disciples after 1772. The hasidism entry (a school) exists.

Shneur Zalman of Liady, founder of the Chabad (Lubavitch) school of Hasidism and author of the Tanya, was among the Maggid's disciples. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created figure entity (flagged as dangling in this batch).
Levi Yitzchak of Berdichev, one of the best-known figures of the second Hasidic generation, was a disciple of the Maggid. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created figure entity.

Elimelech of Lizhensk (Lezajsk), author of No'am Elimelech and a formulator of the doctrine of the tzaddik, was a disciple of the Maggid who spread Hasidism in Galicia. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created figure entity.

Devekut, cleaving or continuous attachment to God, is a recurrent theme of the homilies gathered under the Maggid's name and is developed in his circle alongside the annihilation of the self (bittul); stated as his attributed teaching. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created concept.

The Maggid's contemplative teaching (tzimtzum, the ayin, the ascent of thought to its root) draws on the Kabbalah and especially its Lurianic form, turned toward an inward and acosmic piety; the kabbalah entry exists.

sources.citation: Maggid Devarav le-Yaakov (Maggid Devarav leYaakov, 'The Maggid's Words to Jacob', also known as Likkutei Amarim), the collection of the teachings of Dov Ber, the Maggid of Mezeritch, compiled by his disciple Shlomo of Lutsk and first printed at Korets, 1781; open Hebrew and English text on Sefaria. Cited as the openly-auditable primary text carrying the Maggid's transmitted teaching, with section 1 read line-by-line: God 'contracts Himself, this contraction is called wisdom. Because wisdom is from nothing' (the ayin and tzimtzum); the righteous 'are dust and cannot perform any action without the strength of the Holy One, blessed be He. Thus, what they do, the Holy One, blessed be He, does, for if not for Him, He alone can do nothing' (the annihilation of the separate self, bittul, before the divine All); and 'Israel sustains their Father in Heaven.' These lines were read and verified verbatim as the load-bearing text for the Maggid's contemplative and acosmic teaching.
sources.license: English: Sefaria Community Translation (CC0). Hebrew: public domain (Maggid Devarav leYa'akov, Korets, 1781; digitized from the National Library of Israel). Openly auditable; section 1 read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Schatz-Uffenheimer, Rivka. Hasidism as Mysticism: Quietistic Elements in Eighteenth-Century Hasidic Thought. Translated by Jonathan Chipman. Princeton: Princeton University Press; Jerusalem: Magnes Press, 1993 (Hebrew original, ha-Hasidut ke-Mistikah, Jerusalem, 1968). The standard academic study of the contemplative and quietistic strand of early Hasidism, centred on the Maggid of Mezeritch and his disciples, analyzing the annihilation of the self (bittul), devekut, and the acosmic reading of the divine; cited as the academic-historian lens on the Maggid specifically. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Princeton University Press and JSTOR records; the author was a student of Gershom Scholem and professor of Jewish mysticism at the Hebrew University), not read line-by-line.

Rapoport-Albert, Ada. 'Hasidism after 1772: Structural Continuity and Change.' In Hasidism Reappraised, edited by Ada Rapoport-Albert, 76-140. Oxford: The Littman Library of Jewish Civilization, 1996. A contemporary Jewish scholar's study of how Hasidism developed after the Maggid's death in 1772 from a small circle into an institutionalized movement carried by a dispersal of disciples rather than a single line of succession; cited as the contemporary-Jewish-scholarship lens on the movement's organization and the Maggid's role in it. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Littman Library / Liverpool University Press record), not read line-by-line.
